title: Directory and File Fuzzing
description: Uses a highly optimized Go-based engine to rapidly request URL paths by replacing the 'FUZZ' keyword with entries from a provided wordlist, effectively brute-forcing hidden directories and files.
when_to_use: This is your primary tool for mapping out the hidden attack surface of a web application. Run this immediately against any discovered web server to find admin panels, backups, or undocumented APIs.
common_pitfalls: Failing to filter out false positives. If the server returns a 200 OK for every non-existent page, you must use the '-fc' (filter code) or '-fs' (filter size) flags to hide the noise.

flag_descriptions.-w: Wordlist file path and optional keyword

optional_flags.description: Verbose output, printing full URLs and redirects

title: Virtual Host (VHost) Fuzzing
description: Fuzzes the HTTP 'Host' header to discover subdomains or virtual hosts that are served by the same IP address but aren't publicly listed in DNS.
when_to_use: Crucial when targeting a single IP that might host multiple internal applications (like 'dev.target.local' or 'admin.target.local') that route differently based on the requested Host header.
common_pitfalls: You absolutely must use size filtering ('-fs') or word filtering ('-fw') here, because the default VHost will usually return a valid 200 response, flooding your results.

flag_descriptions.-w: Wordlist file path and optional keyword

optional_flags.description: Verbose output, printing full URLs and redirects

title: Directory Brute-Forcing with Extensions
description: A classic directory brute-forcing tool that allows you to easily append specific file extensions (like .php, .txt, .bak) to every wordlist entry during the scan.
when_to_use: A solid alternative to ffuf when you want a simpler syntax for finding specific file types on a target web server.

title: Configure Proxy Interception
command: FoxyProxy -> Burp (127.0.0.1:8080) | Burp: Proxy -> Intercept is on
description: The fundamental setup for Burp Suite: routing your browser traffic through Burp's local proxy listener so you can pause, inspect, and modify HTTP requests before they reach the server.
when_to_use: Mandatory for all manual web application testing. Use this to bypass client-side validation, analyze hidden API requests, or manipulate session cookies.
common_pitfalls: Forgetting to install PortSwigger's CA certificate in your browser, which will cause all HTTPS sites to throw severe security warnings.

title: Send to Repeater (Shortcut)
description: Quickly sends an intercepted HTTP request to the 'Repeater' tab. Repeater allows you to manually tweak parameters and replay the exact same request endlessly to observe the server's response.
when_to_use: Use this constantly when probing an endpoint for vulnerabilities like SQL Injection, XSS, or IDOR, as it saves you from having to click through the actual website UI over and over.

title: Basic Web Server Vulnerability Scan
description: A classic, noisy web vulnerability scanner that checks web servers for thousands of outdated files, misconfigurations, and known insecure software versions.
when_to_use: Run this in the background early in an engagement. While it generates many false positives, it excels at quickly identifying missing security headers, default index pages, or ancient server software.
common_pitfalls: Extremely noisy and signature-heavy. It will light up a target's Intrusion Detection System instantly.

title: Aggressive WordPress Enumeration
description: A specialized scanner for WordPress environments. This command specifically enumerates vulnerable plugins ('vp'), vulnerable themes ('vt'), and registered users ('u').
when_to_use: The absolute first tool you should reach for whenever you detect a target is running WordPress. Identifying a vulnerable plugin is the most common path to compromising a WP site.
common_pitfalls: Without supplying a free Wpscan API token, the tool will enumerate the plugins but will NOT tell you if they have known vulnerabilities.

title: Automated SQL Injection Testing
description: Automatically tests a URL parameter for SQL injection vulnerabilities. If successful, it exploits the flaw to list all databases ('--dbs') available on the backend server. The '--batch' flag accepts default prompts without user interaction.
when_to_use: Use this when you suspect a URL parameter (e.g., '?id=1') or a POST body field is vulnerable to SQL injection, but you want to automate the tedious payload generation and database dumping process.
common_pitfalls: Can accidentally corrupt database records if testing against fragile production applications. Never run blindly against untested targets.

flag_descriptions.--batch: Never ask for user input, use the default behavior

optional_flags.description: Never ask for user input, use the default behavior
Use randomly selected HTTP User-Agent header value

title: SQLMap via Intercepted Request File
description: Instructs SQLMap to parse a raw HTTP request saved to a text file (usually exported directly from Burp Suite) and target a specific parameter for injection, ultimately attempting to dump the tables.
when_to_use: Crucial when the SQL injection is in a complex POST request, an authenticated session with specific cookies, or hidden within HTTP headers, which are difficult to supply via the command line.

optional_flags.description: Never ask for user input, use the default behavior
Use randomly selected HTTP User-Agent header value
